content:
{
  "title": "Prompt Refusal Ensembles: Adaptive Multi-Perspective Prompting for Robust Language Model Defense",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) are vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attempts that elicit unsafe or policy-violating outputs. Existing prompting strategies are insufficiently robust to prompt obfuscation and multi-turn attacks.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are widely deployed, ensuring their safety against sophisticated adversarial prompting is critical. Recent work shows that attackers can bypass safety guardrails through prompt engineering, necessitating new defense mechanisms. Enhanced prompting could provide a lightweight, deployable solution without requiring model retraining.",
  "method": "We propose a novel adaptive ensemble prompting method that generates multiple, semantically diverse rephrasings of the input prompt using a paraphrase generation model, then queries the target LLM with each. The LLM responses are aggregated, and a refusal classifier analyzes the set for inconsistencies or unsafe completions, triggering an automatic refusal if any prompt variant elicits an unsafe response. This approach leverages the observation that many jailbreaks are sensitive to prompt rewording, so adversarial prompts may fail under paraphrasing. The method is modular and requires no access to LLM internals.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on the AdvBench and JailbreakBench datasets, comparing against vanilla direct prompting and state-of-the-art chain-of-thought and self-consistency prompting. Metrics include attack success rate, unsafe output rate, and refusal rate. A convincing experiment is to demonstrate a significant reduction in attack success rate (at least 40%) over the strongest baseline on unseen adversarial prompts, without sacrificing coverage on benign queries."
}
